questions.question_text: A Delaware landlord collects a security deposit on a two-year lease. What is the maximum amount the landlord may require?
distractors: One and a half months' rent
questions.explanation: For leases of one year or longer in Delaware, the maximum security deposit a landlord may collect is one month's rent. This cap protects tenants from excessive upfront costs while still giving landlords reasonable security against damages.

questions.question_text: A tenant moves out of a Delaware rental on March 1. By what date must the landlord return the security deposit with an itemized statement?
questions.explanation: Delaware law requires landlords to return the security deposit within 20 days of lease termination. The return must include an itemized list of any deductions. Failure to meet this deadline may result in the landlord forfeiting the right to withhold any portion.

questions.question_text: A Delaware landlord wants to keep security deposits in a personal safe at home. Is this permissible?
questions.correct_answer: No, deposits must be in a federally insured institution
distractors: Yes, any secure location is acceptable
Yes, if the tenant agrees in writing
No, deposits must be held in escrow by an attorney
questions.explanation: Delaware requires security deposits to be held in a federally insured financial institution. Keeping deposits in a home safe, personal account, or any non-insured location violates the Landlord-Tenant Code and exposes the landlord to penalties.

questions.question_text: A Delaware tenant with a pet asks if the landlord can charge a pet deposit in addition to the standard security deposit. Which statement is correct?
distractors: Pet deposits are prohibited by state law
Pet fees must come from the security deposit
Only service animal deposits are permitted
questions.explanation: Delaware law permits landlords to collect a separate pet deposit in addition to the standard security deposit. Under 25 Del. C. §5514, the pet deposit may not exceed one month's rent, is applied first to pet damage, and may not be charged for a duly certified support animal.

questions.question_text: A month-to-month tenant in Delaware wants to end her tenancy. How much notice must she provide to the landlord?
questions.explanation: Delaware requires either party to a month-to-month tenancy to give 60 days' written notice before termination. This is longer than the 30-day notice common in many other states, so licensees should ensure clients are aware of this requirement.

questions.question_text: A Delaware tenant reports that the heating system has been broken for two weeks and the landlord refuses to repair it. Which remedy is available to the tenant?
questions.correct_answer: Repair and deduct the cost from rent
distractors: Change the locks and exclude the landlord
File a claim in Superior Court only
questions.explanation: Delaware tenants may use the repair-and-deduct remedy under 25 Del. C. §5307: after written notice, if the landlord fails to repair within 30 days (or fails to begin corrective action within 10 days), the tenant may have the work done professionally and deduct the reasonable cost, capped at $400 or half of one month's rent, whichever is less. Because heat is an essential service, 25 Del. C. §5308 also lets the tenant terminate, retain two-thirds of the per diem rent, or obtain substitute housing. Simply stopping all rent payments or excluding the landlord is not a lawful remedy.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a landlord obligation under Delaware's habitability standards?
questions.correct_answer: Maintain working plumbing and electrical systems
distractors: Provide furnished living spaces for tenants
Pay the tenant's utility bills monthly
questions.explanation: Delaware's Landlord-Tenant Code (25 Del. C. §5305) requires landlords to comply with housing codes, provide a unit fit for habitation that does not endanger health or safety, keep common areas clean, make necessary repairs, and maintain electrical, plumbing, and other supplied facilities in good working order. Furnishing, paying tenant utilities, and alarm systems are not statutory habitability requirements.

questions.question_text: A Delaware landlord serves a tenant with a notice for non-payment of rent. How many days does the tenant have before eviction proceedings may begin?
questions.explanation: In Delaware, a landlord must give 5 days' written notice for non-payment of rent before filing for summary possession. This gives the tenant a brief opportunity to pay the overdue amount. The eviction is then heard in Justice of the Peace Court.

questions.question_text: A Delaware landlord discovers a lease violation other than non-payment. She wants to begin the eviction process. What notice period must she provide?
questions.explanation: For lease violations other than non-payment, Delaware law requires a 7-day notice to cure. If the tenant fails to remedy the violation within that period, the landlord may then file for summary possession in Justice of the Peace Court.

questions.question_text: A frustrated Delaware landlord changes the locks on a tenant's unit while the tenant is at work, hoping to force the tenant out. What is the legal consequence?
questions.correct_answer: The lockout is illegal self-help eviction
distractors: The lockout is valid with 24-hour notice
The lockout is legal if rent is overdue
The lockout is permitted after a written warning
questions.explanation: Self-help evictions, including changing locks or removing a tenant's belongings, are illegal in Delaware regardless of the circumstances. Landlords must use the formal summary possession process through Justice of the Peace Court. Tenants subjected to self-help evictions can seek damages.
